metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Leaf AC3's era-validity rule formula omits the `revoked_at_commit is None` (null → still valid) branch that the parent's 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section and YAML schema explicitly specify (`revoked_at_commit: "<git-sha-on-main>|null"`). The leaf writes 'a key is valid … iff is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P) and not is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)' — which, read literally, treats null/absent revoked_at_commit as failing the validity check, directly contradicting the parent schema where null means 'still valid'.
findings.location: Leaf AC3 era-validity formula; parent 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' / YAML schema
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC3 validity formula to: 'a key is valid at the cert's bound `main` commit `P` iff `is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P)` and (`revoked_at_commit is None` OR NOT `is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)`)' — matching the parent's "Trust config & era-validity protocol" exactly. Add a test case for the null/still-active path alongside the existing rotation-expiry test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or strengthen AC3) that verifies the shared ancestry predicate is implemented as a single reusable unit (e.g., a named function in a shared module imported by both authorship and opcert), not a copy-pasted duplicate.
findings.finding: The Context describes an explicit structural deliverable — 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate' — but no AC verifies this factoring. AC3 only requires that the era rule be 'reused' and tests its observable output; an implementation that copy-pastes the predicate would pass all ACs while violating the stated design intent.
findings.location: Context paragraph 2 ('factored so authorship… share one ancestry predicate') vs. AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The ancestry comparison (`is_ancestor(a, b)`) lives in a single shared function (e.g., `attest._era.is_ancestor`) imported by both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py`; a test or import assertion confirms both modules reference the same callable.'

findings.finding: The Context explicitly describes factoring a shared ancestry predicate so that both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py` use one function, but no Acceptance Criterion covers the creation of this shared predicate or the corresponding update to `authorship.py`. An implementer could skip the factoring (duplicating the logic instead) and all three ACs would still pass.
findings.location: Context ('factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate') vs AC3 ('reusing the shared ancestry *rule*')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'The shared ancestry predicate is extracted as a named function (e.g. `rebar.attest._era.is_key_valid_at_commit(added_at, revoked_at, position)`) in a shared module; `authorship.py`'s era check is refactored to call it; a test imports both authorship and opcert era logic and asserts they resolve to the same callable.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` to the explicit edit targets: extract the private `_is_ancestor` closure into a module-level (or package-level) exported helper so `opcert.py` can import and reuse it without duplicating the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` subprocess logic.
findings.finding: `authorship.py` is an unacknowledged edit target: the plan calls for factoring the `_is_ancestor` closure (currently private inside `verify_authorship_at_commit`) into a shared ancestry predicate reusable by both `authorship.py` and the new `opcert.py`, but `authorship.py` is not listed as a file the plan touches.
findings.location: Context section: 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'
findings.suggested_fix: Promote `_is_ancestor` (or a thin `is_ancestor(ancestor: str, descendant: str, *, repo_root=None) -> bool` wrapper) to module-level in `authorship.py` (or to a new `src/rebar/attest/_git.py` helper), export it, and have both `verify_authorship_at_commit` and the new `opcert.py` era-check import it. Add `authorship.py` (or `_git.py`) to the plan's edit targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `env_id` passed to `verify_opcert` is supplied by the caller (not read from the DSSE envelope's `keyid`) so the trust-root construction for both phases is pinned independently of attacker-controllable envelope contents — analogous to how `verify_authorship_any_key` receives `identity_id` as an explicit parameter.
findings.finding: The two-phase 'verify against ANY historical key for the env' step needs to select which environment's key records to use; if the env identifier comes from the DSSE envelope's keyid field, that is attacker-influenced data selecting the trust root — the exact alg-confusion the registry's policy-pinning exists to prevent. The plan does not specify how the verifier pins the env identifier independently of the envelope, leaving a potential mechanism hole in the first phase of the two-phase check.
findings.location: Context section, 'Two-phase verdict' paragraph; AC line 3 ('verify against any historical key for the env')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC-3 description: 'The env_id used to select key records for both phases of the two-phase check MUST be passed as an explicit parameter to verify_opcert (not derived from envelope.signatures[0].keyid), mirroring how authorship verification receives identity_id externally.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact verdict token string for the 'not a real key' branch of the two-phase check (underscore 'bad_signature' vs hyphen 'bad-signature') and declare whether it must be added to verify_signature_result.schema.json's verdict enum alongside key_not_valid_at_era.
findings.finding: The plan's two-phase verdict protocol names 'bad_signature' (underscore) as one of two failure verdicts the op-cert verifier must emit, but this token does not appear in any pinned verdict enum: registry.Verdict.verdict is a free string, verify_signature_result.schema.json enumerates [unsigned, foreign_key, certified, mismatch, key_not_valid_at_era], and the existing command-layer constant is BAD_SIGNATURE='bad-signature' (hyphen). The executor must invent whether to use underscore or hyphen form, whether to add it to the schema enum, and how it maps to the registry.Verdict.verdict field — a concrete vocabulary gap.
findings.location: Context section ('two-phase verdict') and AC3 ('else bad_signature')
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'bad_signature' to the verify_signature_result.schema.json enum and to types.py's Literal, and use that underscore form consistently in opcert.py and AC3, matching the underscore convention of key_not_valid_at_era.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the target location and public function signature for the factored _is_ancestor predicate (e.g. rebar.attest._git.is_ancestor(ancestor: str, descendant: str, *, repo_root=None) -> bool) so both authorship.py and opcert.py import the same implementation.
findings.finding: The plan states the _is_ancestor git predicate will be 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate,' but does not specify WHERE the factored function lives (new module? promoted in authorship.py?), its public signature, or its import path. Currently _is_ancestor is a module-private closure inside verify_authorship_at_commit in authorship.py — a decision rule referenced by the new module but not co-located with any schema or API surface the executor can import.
findings.location: Context section ('Environment-key era rotation — explicit-SHA model'), last sentence of that paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Factor the predicate into a new rebar.attest._git module (or promote it as a module-level function in authorship.py with a documented stable name) and reference it explicitly in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `_is_ancestor` is currently a private closure in `authorship.py` and must *be* factored (not already factored); acknowledge the result will have two callers and confirm the lightweight inline-per-module alternative was considered.
findings.finding: The plan presents the shared ancestry predicate as already 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate', but `_is_ancestor` is currently a module-private closure inside `verify_authorship_at_commit` in `authorship.py` with exactly one outer consumer. After this story lands, the factored helper will have exactly two callers (authorship + opcert) — below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a shared abstraction. The abstraction is not harmful (both consumers are in-scope and the helper is trivial), but framing it as 'factored' rather than 'to be factored' obscures that this is a design choice the implementer must make, and the two-caller count means the abstraction is not yet justified by the rule-of-three.
findings.location: Context section — 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'factored so authorship … and op-cert … share one ancestry predicate' to 'to be factored into a shared helper (e.g. `rebar.attest._git.is_ancestor`) that both authorship and op-cert import; note this will have two call-sites at delivery, which is below Rule-of-Three but is accepted because both consumers are in-scope in this story and the helper is trivial.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the op-cert verifier's fallback verdict string with the existing `verify_authorship.py` convention: confirm whether the token should be `bad-signature` (hyphen, matching `BAD_SIGNATURE` in `verify_authorship.py:54`) or `bad_signature` (underscore), and make the AC and Context section consistent with whichever is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan's Context section specifies the op-cert verifier should 'emit `bad_signature`' (underscore) as the fallback verdict string, but the analogous existing gate (`verify_authorship.py`) uses `bad-signature` (hyphen: `BAD_SIGNATURE = 'bad-signature'` at line 54). The plan simultaneously claims the op-cert two-phase check 'matches `verify_authorship.py`', creating a verifiable inconsistency between the specified verdict token and the codebase convention it claims to mirror.
findings.location: Context section — 'Two-phase verdict' paragraph; AC line 3 ('else `bad_signature`')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Context section and AC3 to use `bad_signature` with underscore only if a new shared constant is introduced; otherwise adopt `bad-signature` (hyphen) to match `verify_authorship.py`'s existing `BAD_SIGNATURE = 'bad-signature'` constant and the `verify_signature_result` schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the plan's verdict string for a totally-invalid signature to match the existing gate's `bad-signature` (hyphen) constant from `verify_authorship.py`, or explicitly define a new canonical value and update all consumers; remove the ambiguity between `bad_signature` (underscore) in the plan and `bad-signature` (hyphen) in the codebase.
findings.finding: The plan uses `bad_signature` (underscore) as the verdict string the op-cert verifier must emit, but the existing gate implementation uses `bad-signature` (hyphen) — `BAD_SIGNATURE = 'bad-signature'` in `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:54`. Neither form appears in the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum (which contains only `unsigned`, `foreign_key`, `certified`, `mismatch`, `key_not_valid_at_era`). An implementer following the plan literally will produce a verdict string inconsistent with the existing gate's vocabulary.
findings.location: AC3: 'else `bad_signature`'; Context two-phase verdict section: 'emit `key_not_valid_at_era`; otherwise emit `bad_signature`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `bad_signature` with `bad-signature` in the plan (AC3 and Context), matching `BAD_SIGNATURE = 'bad-signature'` in `verify_authorship.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the complete `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` key-record schema (at minimum: the public-key field name/format, the env identifier field, and the two SHA fields) so the executor can implement and test the key loader without guessing.
findings.finding: The schema of `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` key records is only partially specified: `added_at_commit` and `revoked_at_commit` fields are named, but the field name(s) for the public key material and the env identifier (used to look up the right key set) are never stated, leaving the executor to guess or invent a schema.
findings.location: Context section — 'Environment-key era rotation' paragraph; AC3
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a minimal YAML example to the plan, e.g.:
```yaml
environments:
  - env_id: prod
    public_key: "<base64-ed25519-pubkey>"
    added_at_commit: "<sha>"
    revoked_at_commit: null  # null = still active
```
and name the field `env_id` (or whatever the canonical name is) explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g. `pytest src/rebar/attest/test_opcert.py -v`) to each AC or in a 'Verification' section so done-ness is unambiguously reproducible.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands are specified for any of the three acceptance criteria; the plan describes what tests must assert but not the command an executor or reviewer runs to confirm all ACs are met and produce evidence of success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all three AC bullets
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to each AC or add a Verification section:
```
Proving command: pytest src/rebar/attest/test_opcert.py -v
```
confirming all three assertion groups pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the explicit-SHA era model for environment keys, the `trusted_environments.yaml` schema (required fields, branch location), and why it differs from the authorship position→commit resolver.
findings.finding: The novel explicit-SHA era model for environment-key rotation is a meaningful architectural departure from the authorship (position→commit) model, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is planned to capture it — future maintainers will have no canonical reference for why trusted_environments.yaml uses explicit SHAs, what fields are required, or how the out-of-band trust config interacts with the tickets branch.
findings.location: Context section ('Environment-key era rotation') and AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/opcert-env-key-era-model.md`) covering: (1) why explicit SHAs vs. position→commit, (2) the trusted_environments.yaml schema, (3) which branch it lives on, and (4) the two-phase verdict contract. Reference it from CLAUDE.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for `trusted_environments.yaml` (missing file → explicit error Verdict, not exception), git subprocess failures (non-zero exit, invalid SHA → explicit error Verdict with logged cause), and add at least a structured log entry for every verification failure on the op-cert path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new failure points — reading `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (file I/O) and invoking `git merge-base --is-ancestor` (subprocess) — but specifies no error handling for missing/malformed config, invalid SHAs, or git subprocess failures, and no observability for verification failures on this security-critical path.
findings.location: Context section ('Environment-key era rotation'); AC bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: In opcert.py: wrap yaml load in try/except → return a Verdict('config_error', ...) on failure; wrap git subprocess in try/except subprocess.CalledProcessError → return Verdict('era_check_error', ...); emit a structured log line (logger.warning) on every non-verified verdict with the env_id and reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the verifier's behavior when `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` is absent or malformed (e.g., treat as 'no keys → bad_signature' with a structured error, not an unhandled exception), and add an AC or note covering this consumer-tolerance case.
findings.finding: The plan names `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` as the key store consumed by the op-cert verifier but does not specify what the verifier does when the file is absent, empty, or malformed — the consumer-tolerance sub-criterion for shared file state is unaddressed.
findings.location: Context section — 'Env keys live out-of-band (.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml…)'; AC3 — verifier consumes this file for era-validity checks
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Context or AC3: 'If trusted_environments.yaml is absent or unparseable, verify_opcert returns bad_signature (not a raised exception); a test asserts this.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `register_opcert_policy()` is idempotent (duplicate calls are a no-op or safe overwrite), mirroring whatever contract `authorship.py`'s registration follows.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `register_opcert_policy()` is idempotent (safe to call multiple times), which matters for test isolation and for any module that imports `attest` more than once in a process.
findings.location: AC1 — 'register_opcert_policy() pinning rebar.opcert.v1 → sshsig in registry.POLICY'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'register_opcert_policy() uses dict assignment (POLICY[namespace] = scheme), making it idempotent by construction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC3 test assertion for the `bad_signature` verdict: sign a cert with a key that appears in NO historical key record (or tamper the signature bytes) and assert the verifier returns `bad_signature`, not `key_not_valid_at_era`.
findings.finding: The two-phase verdict logic explicitly defines two branches — `key_not_valid_at_era` (era check fails after signature passes) and `bad_signature` (no valid historical key matches) — but the stated ACs only assert a test for the `key_not_valid_at_era` branch; the `bad_signature` branch (tampered/invalid signature with no matching historical key) has no named test assertion.
findings.location: AC3 / Context 'Two-phase verdict' section
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: 'A third assertion signs with a wholly-unknown key (not in `trusted_environments.yaml`) and verifies the verifier returns `bad_signature`; a fourth assertion tampers the signature bytes of a cert from a valid key and asserts `bad_signature`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the specific gate call sites outside `src/rebar/attest/` that must be updated to call `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert` (at minimum `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py`) or explicitly declare wiring those callers is deferred to a separate child and is out of scope for this story.
findings.finding: The plan names 'the plan-review / completion-verifier gate' as the caller that will invoke `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert`, but does not enumerate the specific files outside `src/rebar/attest/` that will need updating — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`, `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py`, and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` all currently compute `material_fingerprint` and build attestation payloads via the HMAC path.
findings.location: Context: 'the caller (the plan-review / completion-verifier gate) supplies it'; AC1: 'Op-certs are produced/verified via the foundation substrate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for using the sshsig/DSSE substrate (rather than a pure-Python Ed25519 primitive) for op-certs — e.g., no new runtime dependency, alg-confusion resistance from policy-pinned dispatch, and confirmed ssh-keygen availability in the target gate environments.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing the sshsig/DSSE substrate (vs. a direct Ed25519 library call, e.g. PyNaCl or cryptography.hazmat) for environment op-certs. The plan re-uses the substrate by analogy ('mirroring authorship.py') but never explicitly justifies why the subprocess/ssh-keygen path is preferred for env certs — which run in automated gate contexts where ssh-keygen availability is not guaranteed in the same way as a developer's workstation.
findings.location: Context section, first paragraph ('mirroring attest/authorship.py'); AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete field schema for a trusted_environments.yaml key record, including the public-key field name (e.g. 'public_key'), the environment-id field name, and whether multiple keys per environment are allowed.
findings.finding: The trusted_environments.yaml key-record schema names only 'added_at_commit' and 'revoked_at_commit' fields; the field name(s) for the public key bytes (the Ed25519 public key the verifier must build its trust root from) are never stated. The opcert.py verifier must parse these records to construct an allowed_signers-equivalent trust root, so the missing field name is a structural schema gap the executor must invent.
findings.location: Context section ('Environment-key era rotation') and AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fail-closed fallback for trusted_environments.yaml load failures (absent file, parse error, empty key list) — e.g. 'return a non-verified Verdict with verdict="unknown_principal" and never raise, mirroring authorship.py's contract'.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what the op-cert verifier does when .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml is absent, empty, or malformed — a fail-closed fallback that authorship.py specifies exhaustively (returning non-verified Verdicts with 'unknown_principal' on any lookup/I/O failure). For a security-critical component the absence of a specified fallback protocol is a structural gap the executor must invent, risking an exception-equals-pass bug.
findings.location: Context section ('Environment-key era rotation') and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: 'If trusted_environments.yaml is absent or unparseable, verify_opcert returns Verdict(verified=False, verdict="unknown_principal", reason="…") and never raises (fail-closed, mirroring authorship.py).'

findings.finding: The 'material fingerprint' field named in the AC2 in-toto Statement subject binding is undefined: the plan does not specify what content is hashed (ticket description? acceptance criteria? the event log? a canonical JSON snapshot?). The existing `material_fingerprint` function in `plan_review/attest.py` is plan-review-specific and depends on `PlanContext`/child tickets — not reusable for opcert subjects without explicit guidance.
findings.location: AC2 — 'The certificate subject is an in-toto v1 Statement binding {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
